h1Title: Testicular Atrophy: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Testicular atrophy is reduction in testicular volume below the normal threshold of 15 mL per testis in adults, caused by loss of germinal epithelium, Leydig cells, or both. The condition can arise from vascular compromise, prior orchitis, hormonal suppression, varicocele, trauma, or cryptorchidism, and manifests clinically as a small, soft testis with impaired sperm production, reduced testosterone, or both. Estimated prevalence in male infertility cohorts is 5-10%, and the condition contributes to 30-50% of cases of non-obstructive azoospermia. Unilateral atrophy spares fertility and endocrine function in most men, but bilateral atrophy carries high rates of hypogonadism and azoospermia. Treatment depends on the underlying cause; testosterone replacement addresses hypogonadism while sperm retrieval and assisted reproduction remain the fertility options for bilateral testicular failure.

keyStats.prevalence: Testicular atrophy is found in approximately 5-10% of men undergoing infertility evaluation; varicocele-related atrophy is the most common reversible cause in adolescents and young adults
keyStats.demographics: Affects males of all ages from neonatal (cryptorchidism) through adult (post-orchitis, post-varicocele); bilateral atrophy predominantly in men aged 30-55
keyStats.avgAge: Post-orchitis atrophy typically presents in adult investigation 5-15 years after the infectious event; varicocele-related atrophy detected at any age from puberty
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated global prevalence not precisely known; indirect estimate from WHO male infertility data suggests several million affected men worldwide

definition: Testicular atrophy (ICD-10: N50.0) is defined as a reduction in testicular size below 15 mL per testis by orchidometer or below 4 cm in longest axis by scrotal ultrasound, with corresponding histological evidence of reduced spermatogonial density, tubular hyalinization, or loss of Leydig cell mass. The testis has two principal functional compartments: the seminiferous tubules, producing sperm under FSH and testosterone stimulation, and the interstitial Leydig cells, producing testosterone in response to LH. Atrophy may be germinal (affecting sperm production only, with preserved testosterone), endocrine (affecting Leydig cells and testosterone production), or combined. Causes include prior orchitis (viral or bacterial), testicular torsion with ischemia, cryptorchidism, varicocele-associated venous hypertension, exogenous androgen suppression, irradiation, and chemotherapy. Unilateral atrophy often compensates via contralateral hypertrophy maintaining normal hormonal and fertility function. Bilateral atrophy causes primary hypogonadism with elevated LH and FSH, reduced testosterone, and typically azoospermia or severe oligozoospermia. Urology and andrology specialists manage the fertility implications; endocrinology is involved when testosterone deficiency requires replacement therapy.

typesClassification.description: Loss of spermatogenic cells with relative preservation of Leydig cell function. FSH is elevated; testosterone normal or low-normal. Causes include viral orchitis, cryptorchidism, and varicocele. Fertility is severely impaired; endocrine function less so.

Reduction in Leydig cell mass and testosterone production. LH is elevated; testosterone low. Causes include severe bilateral orchitis, irradiation, and autoimmune orchitis. Results in primary hypogonadism with all its systemic consequences.

Loss of both spermatogenesis and Leydig cell function, the most severe form. Seen in bilateral mumps orchitis, bilateral torsion, or post-irradiation and chemotherapy. Requires both testosterone replacement and assisted reproduction planning.

One testis atrophied, the contralateral testis hypertrophies to near-normal combined volume. Fertility and testosterone are often maintained. Clinical importance is in detecting the underlying etiology and monitoring for malignant transformation in the atrophic testis.

primarySymptoms: Visibly or palpably smaller testis compared to the contralateral side or to prior examination — the primary clinical finding, often first noticed during self-examination or by a partner.
Soft, doughy testicular consistency instead of normal firm rubbery texture, indicating loss of spermatogenic cell mass and tubular structure.
Reduced ejaculatory volume and change in semen appearance from decreased sperm concentration, though these findings require semen analysis to confirm.
Signs of testosterone deficiency in bilateral atrophy — fatigue, reduced libido, erectile dysfunction, loss of morning erections, and decreased muscle mass.
Infertility or failure to conceive after 12 months of unprotected intercourse when bilateral atrophy has impaired spermatogenesis below fertile thresholds.
Scrotal heaviness or a dull aching discomfort, particularly when the underlying cause is varicocele or residual epididymo-orchitis.
Hot flushes, mood changes, and reduced bone density in men with established primary hypogonadism from bilateral atrophy.
Prior history of painful testicular swelling (orchitis) or acute scrotal pain (torsion) that often precedes the discovery of atrophy by months to years.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressive reduction in testicular volume on serial orchidometry or ultrasound in a young man with varicocele — catch-up growth failure or ongoing atrophy indicates the need for varicocele repair.
Testicular softening or reduction in size noticed during routine self-examination 3-6 months after an episode of orchitis.
Elevated serum FSH on routine fertility evaluation, indicating early loss of spermatogenic cell mass before volume reduction is clinically apparent.
Asymmetric testicular size — one testis more than 20% smaller than the other — which warrants ultrasound evaluation to quantify volume and assess parenchymal architecture.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of severe testicular pain with rapid swelling in a young male — testicular torsion must be excluded immediately, as torsion lasting more than 6 hours causes ischemic atrophy with >50% salvage failure by 24 hours.
High fever, bilateral testicular swelling, and systemic symptoms in a post-pubertal male — bilateral orchitis threatening bilateral testicular function requires urgent assessment.

New firm testicular nodule in an atrophied testis — atrophic testes carry an approximately 2-4-fold elevated risk of testicular germ cell tumor compared with the normal population; urgent ultrasound and tumor markers are required.

causes.cause: Orchitis from viral or bacterial infection
causes.description: Mumps orchitis is the most studied infectious cause, producing bilateral atrophy in 30-40% of men with bilateral orchitis from mumps. Bacterial epididymo-orchitis from E. coli, chlamydia, or gonorrhea can also damage the testicular parenchyma when infection penetrates the blood-testis barrier. Inflammation triggers apoptosis of spermatogonia and Leydig cell damage.

Twisting of the spermatic cord obstructs testicular blood supply. Duration of ischemia determines outcome: the testis salvage rate falls from >90% at 6 hours to <10% at 24 hours. Atrophy of the detorsed (salvaged but ischemic) testis occurs in 40-70% of cases with torsion duration above 6 hours as progressive loss of spermatogonia follows reperfusion injury.

Abnormally dilated pampiniform plexus veins create retrograde venous blood flow, elevating scrotal temperature by 1-2 °C and increasing reactive oxygen species and intratesticular pressure. Grade III varicocele reduces ipsilateral testicular volume by 10-30% and is the most common reversible cause of testicular atrophy in adolescents and young adults.

The undescended testis is exposed to higher core body temperature (37 °C vs 34 °C in the scrotum), causing progressive germ cell apoptosis from age 2-3 years onward. By adulthood, untreated unilateral cryptorchidism produces a small, atrophied testis with severely impaired spermatogenesis. Orchiopexy before age 1-2 years substantially reduces but does not eliminate progressive atrophy.

Supraphysiological testosterone from anabolic steroids suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis via negative feedback, reducing LH and FSH to near zero. Intratesticular testosterone falls by 95% and spermatogenesis ceases within 4-8 weeks of use, accompanied by bilateral testicular volume reduction averaging 25-30% in controlled studies. Typically reversible within 6-18 months of stopping, though recovery may be incomplete after prolonged use.

Ionizing radiation damages the radiosensitive spermatogonial stem cells; doses as low as 0.1 Gy impair spermatogenesis; doses above 6 Gy to both testes cause permanent azoospermia and severe atrophy in most men. Alkylating chemotherapy agents (cyclophosphamide, busulfan) are most gonadotoxic. Sperm cryopreservation before treatment is the standard of care.

riskFactors.factor: History of mumps orchitis in post-pubertal men
riskFactors.description: Bilateral mumps orchitis produces severe atrophy and azoospermia in approximately 30-40% of affected men; unilateral orchitis causes atrophy but usually preserves fertility. Unvaccinated men born before universal MMR vaccination represent a cohort at ongoing risk.

Clinical varicocele is present in 35-40% of men with primary infertility and is the most prevalent reversible cause of progressive testicular atrophy. Repair before significant volume loss occurs (total testicular volume >20 mL on ultrasound) achieves return of normal volume in 70-80% of adolescents.

The ipsilateral testis undergoes ischemic atrophy in 40-70% of surgically corrected torsions when ischemia lasted more than 6 hours; contralateral atrophy also occurs in 10-20% over the following year from an immunological mechanism.

Undescended testes that are not surgically corrected by early childhood progress to significant germinal atrophy. The risk of atrophy and fertility impairment rises substantially if orchiopexy is deferred past age 2 years.

Estimated 3-4 million anabolic steroid users in the United States; prolonged use causes bilateral testicular volume reduction averaging 25-30%. Typically reversible after cessation but recovery may take 6-18 months.

riskFactors.factor: Prior radiotherapy or chemotherapy involving gonadal field
riskFactors.description: Scatter radiation doses of 0.1 Gy impair spermatogenesis; direct doses above 6 Gy produce permanent atrophy. Sperm banking before treatment is the key modifiable preventive strategy.

Anti-sperm antibodies and T-cell-mediated destruction of the seminiferous epithelium, sometimes associated with systemic autoimmune diseases or following testicular biopsy; accounts for fewer than 5% of testicular atrophy cases.

affectedDemographics: Adolescent males with varicocele develop progressive ipsilateral testicular hypotrophy; varicocele affects 15-20% of adolescent males and 35-40% of infertile men.
Post-pubertal men who contracted mumps before universal MMR vaccination (largely those born before 1980 in most high-income countries) carry residual risk of bilateral atrophy from prior orchitis.
Men who have undergone testicular torsion, particularly those in whom surgical correction was delayed beyond 6 hours, develop ipsilateral atrophy in up to 70% of cases.

Male cancer survivors who received radiotherapy, alkylating agents, or both represent a growing population with post-treatment testicular atrophy; estimated 50,000-100,000 male cancer survivors per year in the US are affected.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnostic evaluation begins with a detailed history: childhood scrotal surgery, orchiopexy, orchitis, torsion, medication use (particularly anabolic steroids), cancer treatment, and fertility concerns. Physical examination with an orchidometer quantifies testicular volume bilaterally: adult normal is ≥15 mL per testis; atrophy is defined as <15 mL or a size discrepancy of more than 20% between sides. Scrotal ultrasound is the primary imaging tool, measuring testicular dimensions in three planes (volume = length × width × height × 0.71) and assessing parenchymal echogenicity. Heterogeneous echogenicity, microcalcifications, or hypoechoic nodules require further evaluation to exclude germ-cell tumor. Doppler ultrasound assesses vascularity and is essential after suspected torsion. Hormonal evaluation includes serum FSH, LH, total testosterone (morning sample, 8-10 a.m.), and prolactin. Elevated FSH with low-normal testosterone indicates germinal atrophy; elevated both LH and FSH with frankly low testosterone indicates Leydig cell failure (primary hypogonadism). Semen analysis is performed to quantify the fertility impact: oligozoospermia, cryptozoospermia, or azoospermia determine ART planning. Testicular biopsy provides definitive histology (seminiferous tubule diameter, spermatogenic cell density, Sertoli-only pattern, Leydig cell count) and is typically combined with sperm retrieval for cryopreservation when micro-TESE is planned. In cases of bilateral atrophy with primary hypogonadism, bone densitometry assesses osteoporosis risk.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies testicular volume bilaterally and assesses parenchymal architecture, vascularity, and presence of varicocele or neoplasm
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Physical examination with a Prader orchidometer takes 2 minutes; scrotal ultrasound 15-20 minutes. Ultrasound is painless, using gel and a high-frequency probe. Volumes below 15 mL per testis are diagnostic of atrophy. Doppler images the vascular supply.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum FSH, LH, and morning total testosterone
diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes the hormonal pattern — elevated FSH indicates germinal atrophy; elevated LH and low testosterone indicate Leydig cell failure
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw before 10 a.m. (testosterone is diurnal, peak in morning). Results within 24-48 hours. Two morning samples 30 minutes apart improve accuracy for testosterone diagnosis per Endocrine Society guidelines.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies spermatogenesis output — azoospermia, oligozoospermia, or normal sperm concentration guides fertility planning
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Masturbation sample after 2-7 days abstinence; examined by the lab within 1 hour. WHO 2021 reference values: sperm concentration ≥16 million/mL, total motility ≥42%, progressive motility ≥30%. Atrophic testes often produce azoospermia or cryptozoospermia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates venous reflux and varicocele grade; detects absent or reduced arterial flow after torsion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Color-flow Doppler added to routine scrotal ultrasound; venous reflux above 0.5 m/sec on Valsalva confirms clinical varicocele. Takes an extra 5-10 minutes during the standard ultrasound.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for germ-cell tumor in atrophic testis, which carries 2-4-fold elevated cancer risk compared with the general population
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 24-48 hours. Elevated AFP or HCG in association with a testicular mass requires urgent urology referral for possible orchiectomy and staging.

diagnosticTests.test: Testicular biopsy with simultaneous sperm extraction (micro-TESE)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides histological diagnosis (tubular atrophy grade, presence of residual spermatogenesis) and retrieves sperm for ART
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case procedure under local anesthesia or light sedation; a small incision in the scrotum. Under optical magnification, larger tubules are identified and sampled. Mild discomfort for 2-3 days; scrotal support recommended for 1 week.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of testicular atrophy targets the underlying cause when reversible and addresses the functional consequences (hypogonadism and infertility) when structural damage is established. For varicocele-related atrophy in adolescents, microsurgical varicocele repair (varicocelectomy) achieves testicular catch-up growth in 70-80% of properly selected cases (testicular volume discrepancy >20%) and is the recommended intervention per AUA and EAU guidelines when atrophy is documented and progressing. For anabolic steroid-induced atrophy, stopping the offending agent and supporting hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis recovery with human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG 1500-5000 IU three times weekly for 3-6 months) or selective estrogen receptor modulators (clomiphene citrate 25 mg daily) accelerates recovery of testosterone and testicular volume. For bilateral testicular atrophy causing primary hypogonadism, testosterone replacement therapy (TRT) relieves symptoms of testosterone deficiency — fatigue, reduced libido, erectile dysfunction — but suppresses gonadotropins and further reduces testicular volume and residual spermatogenesis. Men who wish to preserve fertility or sperm production should use hCG-based therapy or LH/FSH stimulation rather than TRT. For post-orchitis or post-torsion atrophy where spermatogenesis is severely impaired, micro-TESE combined with IVF-ICSI offers the best chance of biological fatherhood, with sperm retrieval rates of 30-50% in carefully selected cases of non-obstructive azoospermia. Antioxidant therapy — vitamin C 1000 mg, vitamin E 400 IU, coenzyme Q10 200 mg daily — reduces reactive oxygen species generated within the atrophic testis and modestly improves sperm parameters in men with oligozoospermia. Surveillance of the atrophic testis for malignant transformation with annual scrotal ultrasound and annual serum AFP/HCG is recommended for men with bilateral atrophy, prior cryptorchidism, or known increased germ-cell tumor risk.

medicalTreatments.name: Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) 1500-5000 IU subcutaneously three times weekly
medicalTreatments.description: Mimics LH action to stimulate Leydig cell testosterone production; used instead of exogenous testosterone when fertility preservation is desired. Also used to restore testicular volume and spermatogenesis after anabolic steroid suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores serum testosterone to normal range in 70-85% of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism cases; testicular volume recovery in 60-80% when started within 12 months of suppression

medicalTreatments.name: Clomiphene citrate 25-50 mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that blocks hypothalamic negative feedback, increasing LH and FSH and thereby stimulating endogenous testosterone production and spermatogenesis. Used in secondary hypogonadism or as post-anabolic steroid recovery agent.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Raises testosterone levels by 50-100% above baseline in most men with secondary hypogonadism; returns sperm to the ejaculate in 30-50% of hypogonadotropic azoospermia cases

medicalTreatments.name: Testosterone replacement therapy (TRT) — gel, injection, or patch
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated for bilateral primary hypogonadism when fertility is not desired. Relieves all symptoms of testosterone deficiency. Not used when fertility preservation is the goal as it suppresses spermatogenesis. Available as daily topical gel, biweekly or monthly intramuscular injection, or implanted pellets.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalizes serum testosterone in >90% of patients on appropriate dosing; improves libido, erectile function, energy, muscle mass, and bone mineral density

medicalTreatments.name: Antioxidant supplementation (vitamin C, E, coenzyme Q10, selenium)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces intratesticular reactive oxygen species that accelerate spermatogenic cell apoptosis in atrophic testes. A 2019 Cochrane review found modest improvement in sperm parameters but insufficient evidence to recommend routine use for azoospermia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves total sperm count by approximately 20-30% and motility by 5-10% in oligozoospermic men in randomized trials; benefit in azoospermia is uncertain

medicalTreatments.name: Gonadotropin therapy (FSH + hCG) for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism
medicalTreatments.description: Combined FSH (75-150 IU subcutaneously three times weekly) plus hCG stimulates both spermatogenesis and testosterone production in men with secondary hypogonadism (Kallmann syndrome, pituitary disease). Often applicable when anabolic steroid suppression is the underlying cause.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Return of sperm to ejaculate in 80-90% of men with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism after 12-24 months of combined therapy

surgicalOptions.name: Microsurgical varicocelectomy
surgicalOptions.description: High inguinal or subinguinal microsurgical ligation of all internal spermatic veins while preserving the testicular artery and lymphatics. The gold-standard approach for varicocele-related testicular atrophy with documented progression. Performed under operating microscope magnification.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Testicular catch-up growth in 70-80% of adolescents with progressive atrophy; sperm parameter improvement in 60-70% of infertile men; natural pregnancy in 40-50% at 1 year (Cochrane meta-analysis 2012)

surgicalOptions.name: Microdissection TESE (micro-TESE)
surgicalOptions.description: Bilateral testicular incisions under magnification to identify isolated foci of spermatogenesis within severely atrophic testes. The procedure of choice for non-obstructive azoospermia including post-orchitis, post-torsion, and post-treatment atrophy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Sperm retrieval in 30-50% of men with non-obstructive azoospermia; significantly better than conventional TESE by approximately 30%

surgicalOptions.name: Testicular prosthesis implantation
surgicalOptions.description: Saline-filled silicone prosthesis placed into the scrotum after orchiectomy or as cosmetic treatment for a small, non-functional atrophic testis. Improves body image and psychological well-being.
surgicalOptions.successRate: High patient satisfaction rates (85-90%) in series; low complication rates (<5% extrusion or infection)

alternativeTreatments.name: Sperm cryopreservation before gonadotoxic therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Banking sperm before chemotherapy, radiotherapy, or bilateral orchiectomy is the most effective strategy for preserving future fertility. Even one cryopreservation session before cancer treatment can protect biological fatherhood when testicular recovery is incomplete.

whySeeSpecialist: A urological andrologist should evaluate all men with progressive testicular atrophy, as the differential diagnosis spans malignancy, varicocele, and hormonal disorders — each requiring specific intervention. Fertility planning requires coordinated care between andrology, reproductive endocrinology, and embryology.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a urologist with subspecialty training in male infertility and microsurgery, ideally at a center affiliated with an IVF laboratory. For testosterone deficiency management, an endocrinologist or reproductive endocrinologist offers hormonal expertise. Ensure the center provides testicular tumor surveillance alongside fertility services.
hospitalCriteria: Acute bilateral orchitis with systemic sepsis requiring intravenous antibiotics
Testicular torsion — a surgical emergency requiring immediate theater access for scrotal exploration
Suspected testicular tumor with elevated serum AFP, HCG, or suspicious ultrasound findings
Micro-TESE or varicocelectomy under general anesthesia
keyFacilities: Male infertility andrology clinics with microsurgical operating capability
IVF centres with embryology laboratory for micro-TESE and ICSI
Endocrinology clinic for primary hypogonadism management
Scrotal ultrasound and Doppler vascular imaging unit
preventionStrategies: Vaccinate against mumps with MMR vaccine — prevents the commonest viral cause of post-infectious testicular atrophy.

Diagnose and repair varicocele in adolescent males with documented progressive testicular volume loss before irreversible atrophy is established; guidelines recommend repair when volume discrepancy exceeds 20%.
Seek immediate surgical evaluation (within 4-6 hours) for sudden testicular pain — every hour of torsion delay reduces salvage rate and increases risk of ischemic atrophy.
Perform orchiopexy for cryptorchidism before age 12-18 months to preserve spermatogenic potential.
Cryopreserve sperm before any gonadotoxic cancer therapy; sperm banking is low-cost and highly effective as future fertility insurance.
Avoid exogenous androgens and anabolic steroids; if recreational use has occurred, discontinue and restore the HPG axis with medical therapy under andrological supervision.
lifestyleModifications: Wear supportive underwear and a scrotal support for 2-4 weeks after any scrotal surgery to reduce hematoma risk and discomfort.
Avoid heat exposure — hot tubs, saunas, laptops on lap — which raises scrotal temperature and impairs residual spermatogenesis.
Maintain a healthy weight; obesity is associated with secondary hypogonadism and increased aromatization of testosterone to estrogen, impairing HPG axis function.
Perform monthly testicular self-examination to detect size changes, new nodules, or texture changes; atrophic testes carry higher cancer risk.
Attend annual scrotal ultrasound surveillance if bilateral atrophy is established to screen for germ-cell tumor development.

recommended: Foods rich in zinc (oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds) to support Leydig cell testosterone synthesis and spermatogenesis
Antioxidant-rich foods — berries, citrus, nuts, tomatoes — to reduce reactive oxygen species in the atrophic testis
Vitamin D-rich foods (oily fish, egg yolks) and sunlight exposure; deficiency is associated with lower testosterone levels in several observational studies
Selenium-containing foods (Brazil nuts, tuna, whole grains) supporting glutathione peroxidase activity in testicular tissue
avoid: Anabolic steroids and testosterone supplements outside medical supervision, which suppress endogenous HPG axis
Excessive alcohol (>14 units/week), which suppresses LH and testosterone and directly injures Leydig cells
High soy intake in very large amounts — phytoestrogens in soy theoretically compete with androgen receptors but clinical effects at typical dietary doses are minimal

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes/week) maintains healthy testosterone levels. Heavy endurance training above 15 hours/week can transiently suppress LH and testosterone via energy deficiency. Cycling may increase scrotal pressure and temperature; use a padded, pressure-reducing saddle and limit rides to under 3 hours. Resistance training supports testosterone levels and bone health in hypogonadal men.
dailyManagement: If on TRT, apply gel at a consistent time each morning and wash hands; cover the application site to prevent transfer to partners or children.
Monitor serum testosterone, hematocrit, and PSA annually on TRT; elevated hematocrit (>54%) requires dose reduction to reduce thrombotic risk.
Perform monthly testicular self-examination: look and feel for asymmetry, new firmness, or nodules.
Keep a symptom diary during hormonal therapy to track libido, energy, mood, and erectile function against hormone levels.
Attend fertility follow-up with semen analysis if any residual spermatogenesis is being pursued or monitored.
prognosis: Prognosis depends on the underlying cause and whether atrophy is unilateral or bilateral. Unilateral testicular atrophy with a healthy contralateral testis maintains normal testosterone levels in most men and preserves paternity in over 60% — outcomes driven by the remaining testis. Bilateral atrophy producing primary hypogonadism requires lifelong testosterone replacement therapy; with adequate TRT, quality of life, bone mineral density, sexual function, and cardiovascular risk markers normalize. Varicocele-related atrophy repaired before significant volume loss occurs has the best prognosis: 70-80% of adolescents achieve catch-up growth and fertility outcomes in adulthood are comparable to non-varicocele peers. Post-orchitis bilateral atrophy from mumps carries a 30-50% rate of successful sperm retrieval via micro-TESE, offering biological fatherhood to roughly one in three affected men with access to ART. The risk of testicular germ-cell tumor is approximately 2-4-fold elevated in atrophic testes (lifetime risk roughly 1-3%), justifying annual ultrasound surveillance in all men with bilateral atrophy or prior cryptorchidism.
recoveryTimeline: Varicocele repair: testicular volume begins improving within 3-6 months, assessed at 6 and 12 months by ultrasound. Anabolic steroid-induced atrophy: testicular volume recovers over 6-18 months after cessation with HPG axis support. Post-orchitis atrophy is generally not reversible. Fertility assessment 6-12 months after any medical or surgical treatment.
complications: Primary hypogonadism with testosterone deficiency — bilateral atrophy reduces testosterone production, increasing risk of osteoporosis (10-year fracture risk up by 30-40%), anemia, metabolic syndrome, and cardiovascular disease.

Azoospermia or severe oligozoospermia causing infertility — bilateral tubular atrophy is the most common histological finding in non-obstructive azoospermia, present in 10-20% of men at infertility evaluation.
Testicular germ-cell tumor — atrophic and previously undescended testes carry a 2-4-fold elevated lifetime cancer risk; annual ultrasound surveillance is recommended.

Psychological impact — altered body image, impaired masculinity perception, sexual dysfunction, and depression associated with testicular atrophy and infertility in young men; counseling reduces anxiety and improves fertility treatment adherence.

Contralateral sympathetic atrophy — in some men following unilateral torsion or orchitis, the contralateral testis undergoes progressive atrophy through an immunological mechanism, detected by serial ultrasound over 1-2 years.

supportResources.name: American Urological Association — Male Infertility Guidelines
supportResources.description: Evidence-based AUA guidelines on male infertility evaluation including testicular atrophy causes and management.

supportResources.name: Resolve: The National Infertility Association
supportResources.description: Support and education for men and couples navigating male infertility, including testicular failure and ART options.

supportResources.name: European Association of Urology — Male Infertility Guidelines
supportResources.description: EAU clinical practice guidelines covering testicular atrophy, varicocele, and non-obstructive azoospermia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Orchitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Orchitis is the acute inflammatory phase — presenting with fever, testicular pain, and swelling. Testicular atrophy is the long-term structural consequence of severe orchitis, detected months to years later as reduced testicular volume. Orchitis requires acute treatment to minimize the degree of subsequent atrophy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epididymo-orchitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epididymo-orchitis combines acute epididymal and testicular inflammation, causing acute scrotal pain and swelling. Atrophy develops in a subset of men months later if testicular parenchyma was damaged. Distinguishing the acute from the chronic phase changes management from antibiotics (acute) to fertility evaluation and hormonal therapy (atrophy).

confusedWithConditions.name: Azoospermia due to Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Infection-related azoospermia and testicular atrophy frequently coexist but are distinct diagnoses: atrophy describes the structural volume loss, while azoospermia describes the functional spermatogenic outcome. A man can have atrophic testes with residual sperm production (oligozoospermia) or normal-volume testes with Sertoli-cell-only syndrome and azoospermia.

relatedConditions.name: Epididymitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Epididymitis from STIs can co-exist with orchitis and contribute to both obstructive azoospermia and testicular atrophy through combined ductal and parenchymal damage.

relatedConditions.name: Azoospermia due to Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: The two conditions frequently co-exist — testicular atrophy from orchitis reduces sperm output, and severe bilateral atrophy typically produces azoospermia requiring micro-TESE for fertility treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Erectile Dysfunction
relatedConditions.relevance: Bilateral testicular atrophy causing testosterone deficiency is a recognized organic cause of erectile dysfunction; testosterone replacement often improves erectile function alongside other hypogonadal symptoms.

faqs.question: What is testicular atrophy?
faqs.answer: Testicular atrophy is a reduction in testicular size below the normal adult volume of 15 mL, caused by loss of sperm-producing cells, testosterone-producing cells, or both. It can result from infections like mumps, varicocele, torsion, undescended testis, or anabolic steroid use. It may cause infertility and testosterone deficiency depending on whether one or both testes are affected.

faqs.question: What causes testicular atrophy?
faqs.answer: Common causes include mumps orchitis (viral), bacterial epididymo-orchitis, testicular torsion with ischemia, varicocele, cryptorchidism, anabolic steroid use, and radiotherapy or chemotherapy. Varicocele is the most common reversible cause in young men. Mumps orchitis was historically the most common infectious cause before widespread MMR vaccination.

faqs.question: Does testicular atrophy affect fertility?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bilateral testicular atrophy typically causes azoospermia or severe oligozoospermia, making natural conception unlikely. Unilateral atrophy usually preserves fertility when the opposite testis is healthy. Fertility treatment options include microsurgical sperm retrieval (micro-TESE) combined with IVF-ICSI for men with bilateral atrophy.

faqs.question: Does testicular atrophy cause low testosterone?
faqs.answer: Bilateral testicular atrophy from severe orchitis or torsion can reduce Leydig cell mass and lower testosterone, causing primary hypogonadism with elevated LH and FSH. Unilateral atrophy rarely causes low testosterone if the other testis is healthy. Symptoms of low testosterone include fatigue, reduced libido, erectile dysfunction, and muscle loss.

faqs.question: Is testicular atrophy reversible?
faqs.answer: Reversibility depends on the cause. Varicocele-related atrophy can be reversed by microsurgical repair in 70-80% of adolescents. Anabolic steroid-induced atrophy is reversible over 6-18 months after stopping steroids. Post-orchitis and post-torsion atrophy from established cell death are generally permanent, though residual sperm may be retrieved for IVF.

faqs.question: How is testicular atrophy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Orchidometer measurement during physical examination and scrotal ultrasound with Doppler quantify testicular volume and assess vascularity. Blood tests for FSH, LH, and testosterone characterize hormonal function. Semen analysis documents the fertility impact. Together these tests distinguish germinal from endocrine atrophy and guide treatment.

faqs.question: Does mumps cause testicular atrophy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mumps orchitis in post-pubertal men causes testicular atrophy in a significant proportion of those affected. Bilateral orchitis carries the highest risk. MMR vaccination prevents mumps and has nearly eliminated this cause of atrophy in vaccinated populations. Men who had bilateral mumps orchitis before vaccination should seek a fertility assessment.

faqs.question: Can anabolic steroids cause testicular atrophy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anabolic steroids suppress LH and FSH via pituitary negative feedback, reducing intratesticular testosterone and causing bilateral testicular volume to fall by 25-30% on average. Atrophy is typically reversible within 6-18 months of stopping steroids, but recovery may be incomplete after prolonged use or repeated cycles.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for testicular atrophy?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause: varicocele repair for varicocele-related atrophy, hCG or clomiphene to restore the HPG axis after anabolic steroid use, and testosterone replacement for hypogonadism from bilateral failure. Micro-TESE combined with IVF-ICSI offers fertility treatment when sperm production is severely impaired.

faqs.question: Does testicular atrophy increase cancer risk?
faqs.answer: Yes. Atrophic testes carry approximately 2-4 times the risk of germ-cell testicular cancer compared with normal testes. Annual scrotal ultrasound surveillance is recommended for men with bilateral testicular atrophy, prior undescended testis, or known increased cancer risk. Any new hard nodule in an atrophic testis requires urgent evaluation.

faqs.question: Does varicocele cause testicular atrophy?
faqs.answer: Grade II and III varicoceles cause progressive ipsilateral testicular volume reduction in up to 30% of adolescent males by elevating scrotal temperature and venous pressure. This is the most common reversible cause of testicular atrophy and a leading indication for varicocelectomy in adolescents to prevent long-term fertility damage.

faqs.question: What is testicular atrophy after torsion?
faqs.answer: Testicular torsion twists the spermatic cord, cutting off blood supply. If repaired within 6 hours, over 90% of testes survive. Beyond 6 hours, ischemic damage kills spermatogonia and the testis undergoes atrophy over the following weeks to months, shrinking to a small, soft remnant. Early surgery is the only way to prevent this outcome.

faqs.question: Can testicular atrophy be prevented?
faqs.answer: The most preventable causes include: mumps (prevented by MMR vaccination), varicocele atrophy (detected and repaired early), anabolic steroid use (avoidance), and post-torsion atrophy (urgent same-day surgical exploration within 4-6 hours of pain onset). Orchiopexy before age 2 for cryptorchidism reduces but does not eliminate atrophy risk.

faqs.question: Is one small testicle dangerous?
faqs.answer: Unilateral testicular atrophy is not dangerous if the other testis is healthy; testosterone and fertility are usually preserved. The main concern is the 2-4-fold elevated cancer risk in the atrophied testis, which warrants annual ultrasound surveillance. Any new firmness, nodule, or pain in an atrophic testis requires urgent urological evaluation.

faqs.question: What hormones are affected by testicular atrophy?
faqs.answer: Bilateral atrophy from testicular damage (primary hypogonadism) raises LH and FSH (as the pituitary tries to stimulate a failing testis) while testosterone falls below the normal range of 10-35 nmol/L. FSH elevation above twice normal specifically reflects spermatogenic cell loss. Testosterone deficiency leads to fatigue, reduced muscle mass, and sexual dysfunction.

metaTitle: Testicular Atrophy: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Testicular atrophy: reduced testicular size from infection, torsion, or varicocele causing infertility and low testosterone. Causes and treatment reviewed.
